claim: When a Beagle runs away, it is running TO a scent target, not FROM home — escape behavior is approach behavior toward a stimulus, not avoidance of the home environment.
stance_target: Beagle lost-dog movement is scent-approach behavior; the dog is not in distress about leaving home
quoted_support: It must be noted that when a Beagle runs away, he is not running from you or from your house. Owners must learn to not take this behavior personally... When a Beagle runs from home - some refer to this as escaping - and wanders away, the dog is often running TO something.

claim: Beagles have 200 million scent receptors and can detect scent up to 1 mile away; un-neutered males can detect a female in heat from 2-3 miles away — scent detection range explains large wandering radius.
stance_target: Beagle lost-dog search radius is directly linked to olfactory detection range — up to 3 miles from stimulus source
scope_conditions: Beagle breed; neutered vs. un-neutered males
quoted_support: Beagles can easily pick up a scent up to a mile away. A male can pick up the scent emitted from a female in heat from 2 to 3 miles away - and this is commonly the reason that an un-neutered male Beagle will run.

claim: Beagles are also driven to escape by pack hunting instinct — the urge to be with other dogs can sometimes be overwhelming — making proximity to other free-roaming dogs a secondary trigger for wandering.
stance_target: Beagle lost-dog behavior has dual drivers: scent-target pursuit and pack reunification drive
quoted_support: In some cases, a Beagle will run off in search of other dogs - since he is a strong pack hunter - and the urge to be with other dogs can sometimes be overwhelming.

claim: A determined Beagle that has picked up a live scent will find a way under, through, or over a fence — standard residential containment is insufficient once a Beagle locks onto a scent.
stance_target: Once a scent hound engages prey-drive, physical barriers become largely ineffective — movement radius is essentially unlimited
quoted_support: A fence will help to delay the running behavior; but a determined Beagle that has picked up a tantalizing scent will work to find a way under, through or over a fence if he wants to.

claim: Beagle dogs pick up pheromones from other dogs at distance, identifying gender, health status, and mood — meaning a lost Beagle's movement is shaped by a continuous stream of social/environmental scent information.
stance_target: Lost Beagle movement is not random wandering but directional responses to specific scent information in the environment
quoted_support: Dogs pick up the pheromones from other dogs at quite a distance - it tells them the gender, health status and even the mood of the other dog. So, with all of this information being processed by a Beagle at just about all times, one can start to understand the urges and impulses to run.

claim: Lost dog search protocols should cover at least a 20-mile radius for shelters, 5-mile radius for posters, and 15-mile radius for vet/pet shop flyers — because shelters do not all communicate with each other about incoming lost dogs.
stance_target: Effective lost-dog search requires multi-channel outreach across a larger geographic area than intuition suggests
scope_conditions: Beagle lost dog; US context but applicable broadly
quoted_support: Immediately contact your local animal services and all shelters within a 20 mile area - if your Beagle is found but there is no room at a local shelter, he may be transported to one farther away - and not all shelters communicate with each other regarding lost dogs that have run away.
